reasoning: The current effective yield (approx. 6.83%) represents an extraordinarily tight high-yield spread environment (roughly 250 bps over the 10-year Treasury yield of 4.31%). Structurally, there is almost no room for further spread compression, creating a highly asymmetrical setup. Over the short to medium term (3-12 months), yields are very likely to rise because: (1) market expectations have violently repriced to zero Fed rate cuts for 2026, pushing the risk-free floor higher, and (2) any normalization of credit spreads toward historical averages (350-450 bps) will force the effective yield upward. Consequently, our conviction peaks in the 3-6 month window where the 'higher for longer' momentum and spread mean-reversion strongly overlap. Over multi-year horizons (3, 5, and 10 years), predictability decreases as full business cycles introduce the possibility of major rate cuts, so probabilities taper smoothly toward 0.52 to reflect increasing uncertainty.
